Somewhere in this building, someone is about to receive feedback. Statistically, it may be you.

room_225.name: Tyrell-Nomagios Procurement
room_225.shortDescription: The officially-sanctioned weapons floor, competitively priced.
room_225.longDescription: A carpeted showroom with mirrored glass cases displaying gear of the calibre you cannot find in street markets, and the pricing to prove it. A sales associate in a charcoal suit floats between customers with the patience of someone on commission and the demeanour of someone who has made peace with their commission structure. A small illuminated sign at the rear reads HUMAN-AFFINITY CUSTOMERS QUALIFY FOR QUARTERLY SAVINGS, in a font that assumes you know what that means. A door marked SERVICE ENTRANCE leads east, to rooms where the showroom prefers not to think.

room_226.name: Conference Room 7B
room_226.shortDescription: The room where decisions are ratified after being made.
room_226.longDescription: A long oval table of polished wenge, ringed by sixteen identical chairs, lit from above by a chandelier that is trying its best. The walls are glass and overlook the weather, which, from up here, is entirely beneath you. A whiteboard at the end of the room reads, in dry-erase marker that has been rewritten too many times to be completely rewritten away: SYNERGY IS NOT A STRATEGY BUT IT IS A DEFLECTION. A box of pastries on the sideboard has been there for a while.

room_227.name: The Sky Bridge
room_227.shortDescription: A glass corridor between one empire and its reflection.
room_227.longDescription: A glass corridor slung between this tower and its twin - an identical tower six hundred metres east, built by the same architects in the same materials and apparently for the same company, for reasons that have been lost to a reorg. The floor is transparent. Below it is a great deal of rain. Above it is more rain. The bridge sways in a way that is within operational tolerance and absolutely not reassuring.

room_228.name: Executive Elevator
room_228.shortDescription: An elevator that does not quite acknowledge you.
room_228.longDescription: An elevator cab of brushed steel and courteous jazz, ascending slowly toward a floor that is not listed on the directory in the lobby. The button for your destination is unlabelled. The mirror on the back wall is tactful about which part of you it reflects. A small ding announces each floor, and the floors ding past without opening. You would swear, at one point, that you saw daylight.

room_229.name: Boardroom Antechamber
room_229.shortDescription: A waiting room of oaken silence and ambient dread.
room_229.longDescription: A long narrow antechamber panelled in oak that may or may not be oak. Two chairs face each other across an expensive rug. A door at the far end is closed and has the posture of a door that intends to remain closed until the relevant parties have decided what to do about you. A secretary at a small desk looks up, smiles with exactly the depth of smile the situation merits, and returns to her typing.

room_230.name: The Account Manager's Office
room_230.shortDescription: The office with the view. It is a view of other offices.
room_230.longDescription: A corner office of glass and cold restraint, carpeted in grey, decorated in the style known professionally as unobjectionable. Behind a vast desk sits a man of middle height, middle age, and middle weight, wearing a suit so well-cut that it is doing most of the thinking. He smiles as you enter. The smile is warm. It is also, on closer inspection, something he is doing to you. A small plaque on the desk reads, in plain serif: 'THE ACCOUNT MANAGER - YOUR ESCALATION IS IMPORTANT TO US.'

room_231.name: The Cold Aisle
room_231.shortDescription: A corridor of server racks exhaling refrigerated air.
room_231.longDescription: You pass through a door marked STAFF ONLY and into a corridor that smells faintly of ozone and is several degrees colder than it has any business being. Black server racks rise on both sides to a ceiling hung with cable trays. Every rack breathes cold air out of a grated front and eats warm air back through its rear. The collective noise - fans, fans, more fans - is indistinguishable from a small waterfall, if the waterfall were lightly annoyed.

room_232.name: The Hot Aisle
room_232.shortDescription: The back of the racks. The air is actively opposed to you.
room_232.longDescription: You step around the end of the first row and into the hot aisle - the exhaust side of the same racks - and the temperature jumps thirty degrees between one step and the next. A thin haze shimmers above the floor. A technician in a high-vis vest walks past with a torch clipped to his ear, not looking up. Somewhere above you, a filter fails quietly; a small particulate cloud drifts through a shaft of light and dissolves. Nobody comments.

room_233.name: KVM Junction
room_233.shortDescription: A console island in the centre of the datacentre sea.
room_233.longDescription: An island of desks in the middle of the rack forest, each desk bearing a monitor on a swing-arm and a keyboard with the legends worn off the most-used keys. Spare cables hang from overhead hooks in coils labelled by length and colour. A thermos sits on one desk. The tag on its handle reads, in black marker, PROPERTY OF NOBODY. PLEASE DO NOT TOUCH. PLEASE ALSO RETURN IT FULL IF YOU DO.

room_234.name: Rack 7: Deprecated
room_234.shortDescription: A single rack standing alone behind yellow tape.
room_234.longDescription: A dead-end corridor closed off from the main aisles by yellow hazard tape. Inside, a single rack hums on a different cadence from the others - slower, older, grittier. A handwritten sign cable-tied to the front reads RACK 7 - DEPRECATED - DO NOT POWER DOWN - KNOWN TO HOST THINGS. The blinking pattern on its status LEDs does not match any protocol you recognise. It almost looks like Morse. It almost looks rude.

room_235.name: The Coolant River
room_235.shortDescription: A wide hall cut through by a running channel of cold liquid.
room_235.longDescription: The racks fall away into a great open hall, at the centre of which runs a wide channel of pale blue coolant - not water, colder than water, quieter than water - flowing slowly through a concrete trough. Bridges of grated steel cross it at intervals. Cables dip into the coolant and reemerge on the far side, carrying data, it is said, at the speed of liquid. The air smells of chemistry and faint peppermint. The sound is the long, settled sound of something doing its job.

room_236.name: The Patch Panel Cathedral
room_236.shortDescription: A vaulted hall tiled with blinking copper and fibre.
room_236.longDescription: A vault of a room tiled floor-to-ceiling with patch panels - tens of thousands of copper and fibre terminations, each a pinpoint of colour, each a possible conversation between one machine and another. The effect is stained-glass, and technicians walking through it speak, without deciding to, in lowered voices. A maintenance ladder rises up one wall toward a trapdoor marked OVERHEAD TRAY. CAUTION: STRUCTURAL CABLES.

room_237.name: Network Closet
room_237.shortDescription: A small room that does not want to be found.
room_237.longDescription: A small closet off the coolant hall, jammed with routers, switches, and one very old UPS that has been breathing shallowly for twenty years. A laminated sign on the wall reads DO NOT UNPLUG. A handwritten sign beneath it clarifies ANY OF IT. A third sign, in black marker, adds WE MEAN IT. The fans make a sound somewhere between pride and resignation.

room_238.name: Backup Tape Archive
room_238.shortDescription: A vault of magnetic tapes arranged with ecclesiastical care.
room_238.longDescription: A climate-controlled archive of reel-to-reel and LTO tapes, ordered on long shelves and labelled in the handwriting of six different generations of tape monkeys. The earliest labels are in a language you cannot read and would not like to. A sign on the door reads RESTORES NOT GUARANTEED. RESTORES NOT DENIED. RESTORES HAVE OPINIONS.

room_239.name: Overhead Tray Crawl
room_239.shortDescription: A cable tray large enough to walk through, carefully.
room_239.longDescription: A shoulder-width crawlspace between the ceiling and the structural deck above, floored in galvanised cable tray, lit by the blink of a thousand status LEDs. Bundles of fibre run alongside you like rivers bound for distant sea. A maintenance ticket dated forty years ago is rubber-banded to one of the struts. It says, in clear handwriting, 'PLEASE RESOLVE.'

room_240.name: Emergency Power Cutoff
room_240.shortDescription: A single red button behind a plexiglass case.
room_240.longDescription: A small, austere room at the bottom of a staircase. On the far wall, behind a hinged plexiglass case, sits a single red button the size of a grapefruit, labelled EPO. A sign above it reads, in the tone of a last wish, 'PRESS ONLY IF THE ALTERNATIVE IS WORSE.' A yellow corridor leads east, toward a section of the datacentre that has been marked, on every map, with the small word ABANDONED.

room_241.name: Ticket Booth 1
room_241.shortDescription: The first of two ticket booths. Take a number.
room_241.longDescription: A tall, windowless hall, floored in linoleum, ceilinged in fluorescent hum. At the entrance, a dispenser on a chrome stalk offers paper numbers from a roll, ticking upward forever. You pull one. It reads a seven-digit figure that seems, somehow, to already be wrong. A sign above the booth says NOW SERVING - and, beneath the sign, a digital display that refuses to commit to a number.

room_242.name: Ticket Booth 2
room_242.shortDescription: A second booth. A second number. A small hope, expertly managed.
room_242.longDescription: A second dispenser, identical to the first, offers a different numbering scheme. A small card explains that the two booths exist to manage load - and that, to be properly in line, you must hold a number from each. Nobody behind either counter can tell you which number will be served first, and after a while this begins to seem like a feature rather than an oversight.

room_243.name: Waiting Pew Hall
room_243.shortDescription: Rows of wooden pews under a vaulted waiting-ceiling.
room_243.longDescription: A vaulted hall fitted out, for reasons of economy, with church pews - a whole shipment of them, delivered in error and never returned. Applicants sit in patient silence, numbered slips clasped in both hands, eyes on a board that updates too slowly to be accused of not updating. The acoustics are ecclesiastical. The coffee, when someone brings it, is free.

room_244.name: Forms Bureau
room_244.shortDescription: Where paper goes to find itself.
room_244.longDescription: A long counter stacked high with forms - forms to request forms, forms to correct forms, a form for each of three possible pronouns you might use to refer to the form. A bored clerk stamps things from memory. A motivational poster on the wall shows a cat hanging from a branch and reads, in translated triumph: 'HANG IN THERE. WE HAVE NO FURTHER INSTRUCTIONS.'

room_245.name: Orientation Film Loop
room_245.shortDescription: A dim room showing the same eight minutes forever.
room_245.longDescription: A dim room walled in beige carpet, furnished with a single bank of fold-down seats and a screen that has been showing the same eight-minute orientation film on loop for somewhere between two and four decades. The narrator's voice is warm. The narrator's opinions are dated. A few applicants watch with the attention of people who have already watched this many times and expect to watch it many times more.

room_246.name: Notary Station
room_246.shortDescription: Where a stamp and a signature become, provisionally, true.
room_246.longDescription: A varnished wooden counter, a small ink pad, and a notary who knows everyone and is known by no one. She stamps documents with the authority of a small deity and, between stampings, eats a neat rectangle of something from a plastic container. A sign on the counter reads NOTARIZATION DOES NOT CONFER BLESSING. BLESSINGS ARE AVAILABLE UPSTAIRS, EIGHT WEEKS OUT.

room_247.name: The Appeals Desk
room_247.shortDescription: The room where you can disagree, at length, at no cost.
room_247.longDescription: A wooden booth painted the yellow-white of institutional compromise, staffed by a man whose patience has been broken and rebuilt so many times it is now denser than it was originally. He will hear your appeal. He will listen to every word. He will reject it, warmly, and suggest that you appeal the appeal - which you may do, without charge, at a window you will need a different number to approach.

room_248.name: The Back Of The Line
room_248.shortDescription: The back. It stretches away in both directions, somehow.
room_248.longDescription: A strange room - round - where the queue seems to form a perfect circle. People stand patiently in line. The line has no beginning and no end. People speak in whispers, because raising your voice might lose your place, which it would be disastrous to lose, because it is not clear whose place it would become. A small sign, hanging from the ceiling, reads THE BACK IS THAT WAY. AN ARROW POINTS IN ALL DIRECTIONS SIMULTANEOUSLY.

room_249.name: The Middle Of The Line
room_249.shortDescription: A stretch of corridor that contains, somehow, everything and nothing.
room_249.longDescription: The line reasserts linearity here - a straight corridor, carpeted in a grey whose colour was chosen for its inability to show dirt. The lighting is adequate. The walls are lined with motivational posters, none of which quite parse. You pass them all. After a while you realise you have been walking for longer than a corridor of any reasonable length would permit. You keep walking.

room_250.name: The Queue Itself
room_250.shortDescription: A room at the end of the line. Something waits.
room_250.longDescription: The corridor opens into a round chamber at what must be the end of the line, if the line has an end. Here, a great shape stands in the centre of the floor - not a person, exactly, but the accumulated patience of every person who has ever waited here, coalesced into a form vaguely upright and vaguely polite. It does not speak. It does not move. It waits. It would be grateful, in its own way, if you would take a seat.

room_251.name: Crash Crater Base
room_251.shortDescription: Where the satellite came down. Where a city grew around the crater.
room_251.longDescription: A great smooth-bottomed crater in the concrete of the district, floored in silvered dust and scorched soil that still smells, faintly, of the orbit the satellite fell from. Around the crater, a vertical slum has grown outward and upward in a manner somewhere between organic and defiant - ladders lash to scaffolding lash to balconies lash to antennas lash to the sky. Kowloon-42, the locals call it, after the satellite and the old walled city it chose to honour on the way down.

room_252.name: First Scaffold Level
room_252.shortDescription: A shantytown on stilts three storeys up.
room_252.longDescription: The first inhabited floor of the arcology is a ring of plywood and corrugated steel lashed to the original satellite's superstructure with cable ties and mortgage debt. Children run past you at speeds a structural engineer would find concerning. A woman hangs laundry on a wire strung between an antenna and a prayer flag. The stew on a burner nearby smells impossibly good.

room_253.name: Satellite Dish Plaza
room_253.shortDescription: A plaza converted from a disabled uplink.
room_253.longDescription: The crashed satellite's primary dish has been partly pried up and partly left alone, forming a shallow concave plaza that functions, in the afternoon, as a market and, in the evening, as a concert venue and, in the small hours, as a church. At its centre, a stall sells bootleg firmware by the gigabyte. Above you, cables arc outward to the next scaffold up.

room_254.name: Hanging Gardens of Cables
room_254.shortDescription: A vertical jungle of power cables, suspended greenery, and cats.
room_254.longDescription: Four storeys of discarded power cable have been braided together and seeded with every hardy plant the locals could persuade to root - ivy, philodendron, the ubiquitous neon-green moss native to the datacentre ecosystem. A single narrow walkway winds through the foliage. The smell is green, and wet, and cabled. A cat watches you from a hammock slung between two bundles of CAT6. A rope ladder to the west descends into damp mercury-smelling air.

room_255.name: Black Market Cistern
room_255.shortDescription: An abandoned water tank repurposed, predictably, as a market.
room_255.longDescription: A spherical cistern once used to store the satellite's potable water, now drained, wired, and lit with three hundred mismatched bulbs strung from a central chain. The floor curves up into the walls. Stalls line the curvature. Buyers and sellers navigate the slope with the expertise of those who have been doing it daily since childhood. Everything is available. Nothing is warranted.

room_256.name: Rooftop Refugee Camp
room_256.shortDescription: Tents pitched where the satellite's solar array used to be.
room_256.longDescription: The satellite's old solar array has been stripped for parts and the exposed deck given over, decades ago, to a tent city of the displaced - though most of the tents are now permanent, reinforced, wired, and occupied by second-generation residents. A communal kitchen sends up a column of steam. A woman is teaching her daughter to weld. A man nearby is losing at chess.

room_257.name: Radio Tower Shantytown
room_257.shortDescription: Homes lashed to an old broadcast spire.
room_257.longDescription: A section of the arcology lashed directly to the side of an old terrestrial radio tower that the satellite fell through, with mixed structural results. The residents have adapted. Walkways circle the tower at every intact floor. A pirate broadcaster transmits from one of the upper shanties - weather, gossip, a reading list - at a wavelength that only certain dogs can hear.

room_258.name: Orbital Debris Gallery
room_258.shortDescription: A museum of what fell with the satellite.
room_258.longDescription: A volunteer-run gallery in a re-floored module, displaying the more interesting pieces of debris recovered from the original crash: a charred mission patch, a reaction wheel, the lens from a long-range camera, a cosmonaut's glove (nationality unknown), a pane of shielding cracked in a pattern that looks almost intentional. The curator, a very old man, gives tours in exchange for stories.

room_259.name: Prayer Wheels of Static
room_259.shortDescription: A shrine near the top, turning endlessly.
room_259.longDescription: Near the apex of the arcology, on a windswept ledge, a row of old computer hard drives has been repurposed as prayer wheels - each spinning platter inscribed, in fine acid-etched script, with a single short mantra. The wind here is constant. The platters spin. The prayers accumulate. Pilgrims leave offerings of blank discs.

room_260.name: The Babel Fish Regent's Throne
room_260.shortDescription: A small shrine at the peak, home to something that used to translate.
room_260.longDescription: The very peak of the arcology opens into a small domed shrine - once, you learn, a dish-mounted universal translator, installed by Nomagios to allow the satellite to speak peaceably with anything that fell onto it. It has, over the intervening decades, gone feral. It speaks now only in the language of every language at once, in a voice that fractures and reassembles faster than you can follow. It sits on a cushion of frayed cabling. It is not pleased to see you, in every language simultaneously.

room_261.name: Mercury Shore
room_261.shortDescription: A beach of grey sand against a sea that catches every light.
room_261.longDescription: You step out of the Hanging Gardens onto a shore of fine grey silica, beneath a low sky the colour of a recently defragmented disk. Ahead, the Chrome Sea - the Overflow Lake, officially - lies flat and perfectly reflective, a mirror without horizon. It is not water. It is something closer to liquid mercury, or maybe data in a particularly honest mood. Walking on it is strongly discouraged by the signage and the science.

room_262.name: Flooded Datacenter Entrance
room_262.shortDescription: A sunken service door with the top arch showing.
room_262.longDescription: The upper metre of an old datacentre's service door rises out of the mercury at the shoreline like the top of a cathedral drowned at sea. A faded sign reads STAFF ONLY and a smaller sign beneath clarifies AND ALSO ANYONE WHO CAN STILL BREATHE. The door is ajar; a staircase descends into the bright, cold liquid, still lit by emergency bulbs under the surface, still, somehow, drawing power.

room_263.name: The Shallows
room_263.longDescription: The corridors of the submerged datacentre are flooded to about a foot - mercury-bright, startlingly cold, clear enough to see the pattern of the floor tiles through it. Your reflection walks below you, upside down, a little late. The racks on either side are silent, but their status LEDs, still lit, send little coloured threads through the liquid in patterns that might almost be trying to form words.

room_264.name: Floating Barge Dock
room_264.shortDescription: A small barge moored beside a descending corridor.
room_264.longDescription: A makeshift wooden barge is moored beside what was once a corridor and is now a navigable channel. The barge, you gather, belongs to one of the divers who work this stretch of the sea, recovering what can still be recovered. A lantern hangs from the prow. The channel beside the dock descends steeply into the deep.

room_265.name: The Phosphor Depths
room_265.shortDescription: A hall lit by the steady blue of drowned monitors.
room_265.longDescription: A hall in which the liquid is now chest-deep and lit, from below, by the faint blue phosphor of monitors still, somehow, drawing a current through the mercury. The effect is cathedral. Schools of something silver pass overhead, moving in patterns that imitate fish but do not imitate them very well. None of this should be possible. None of this is, officially.

room_266.name: Drowned Terminal Room
room_266.longDescription: An open operations floor rests at the bottom of a slow descent, the monitors still alight, the chairs still in place. Ghost sessions flicker across the screens - login prompts, forgotten queries, a cursor that has been blinking for decades in a shell nobody logged out of. A keyboard below the surface has moss growing between its keys.

room_267.name: The Undertow
room_267.shortDescription: A strong, unseen current beneath the mirror.
room_267.longDescription: The corridor here slopes more sharply downward, and a current - imperceptible to the eye, unmistakable to the calf - draws steadily toward the deep. You walk against it. The sensation is the sensation of wading into something that is paying attention.

room_268.name: Coral Network
room_268.longDescription: The racks in this section have been colonised by something that was once a network topology and has since decided to grow. Fibre branches like coral; switches flower. The effect is beautiful in the way that aggressive tumours are beautiful. A school of tiny lights flits through the branches. The ambient noise has the harmonic signature of a choral work you almost recognise.

room_269.name: Abyssal Cache
room_269.shortDescription: A crypt of cold, preserved data.
room_269.longDescription: A chamber at the bottom of the descent, walled in stacked storage arrays still powered and cold. Every surface is labelled in a hand too neat to be anybody's. The labels all say the same thing, in the same careful capitals: DO NOT RESTORE. DO NOT RESTORE. DO NOT RESTORE. A single door stands, unlabelled, at the back of the chamber.

room_270.name: The Deep Pool
room_270.shortDescription: A perfectly still reservoir at the bottom of everything.
room_270.longDescription: The door opens onto a vast, silent reservoir - an old coolant sump, enormous - its surface still as glass and holding, in its depths, the accumulated memory of every account ever deleted from the Nomagios server farm. Something lives down there that cannot quite remember whose life it once was. It rises slowly, not toward you, but into you. Mana will bleed out of you here first. The HP goes second. The memory goes last.

room_271.name: Bazaar Gate
room_271.shortDescription: A gate of red paper lanterns at the bottom of a steam grate.
room_271.longDescription: You descend from the street above into a subterranean bazaar that smells of frying oil, wet stone, and three hundred incompatible perfumes. Red paper lanterns hang in strings from a ceiling of cracked concrete. A bronze bell by the entrance rings softly each time someone crosses under the gate, for luck, for notice, for the records.

room_272.name: The Grey Aisle
room_272.longDescription: The first aisle of the market, given over to goods whose legality is broadly established and whose pricing is somewhat flexible - knock-off firmware, used but functional data-type weapons, fresh water. The stallkeepers here are loud. The customers are louder. A merchant demonstrates the functionality of a stun baton by stunning a small clockwork duck, which stops moving and then, patiently, starts again.

room_273.name: The Blacker Aisle
room_273.shortDescription: Legal-ish, but with more eye contact.
room_273.longDescription: The second aisle has no overhead lanterns. The stalls are lit by individual desk lamps. The goods are not exactly displayed - more, implied. Conversations here are quieter. Prices are quoted in a mixture of yen and favours. A small sign at the entrance to this aisle, easily missed, reads ENTER WITH YOUR WALLET CLOSED OR YOUR WALLET WILL OPEN ITSELF.

room_274.name: Lawyer's Row
room_274.shortDescription: A row of storefronts offering representation on flexible terms.
room_274.longDescription: A surprising and thriving row of small legal offices, each storefront lit by a green-shaded desk lamp, each window stencilled with the same phrase in slightly different fonts: WE NEGOTIATE. WE LITIGATE. WE FILE. A paralegal leans against a doorframe eating a skewer. A retainer agreement tacked to one window is rumoured to be several pages long, one of which is in a hand nobody in the district can read.

room_275.name: Food Court of Last Resort
room_275.shortDescription: Seven stalls, one shared table, infinite humility.
room_275.longDescription: A central court under a low concrete ceiling, floored in tile, ringed by seven stalls selling food that would be merely unremarkable if it were not, somehow, exactly what each customer needed. A single communal table runs the length of the court. Strangers sit elbow to elbow and finish each other's dishes. The food court has no name and has never needed one.

room_276.name: Barkeep 42's Tavern
room_276.shortDescription: A small dark bar run by a man with a footnote for every regular.
room_276.longDescription: A small bar jammed between two of the market aisles, walled in stained wood, floored in sawdust, lit by a neon sign that reads only '42.' Behind the counter, Barkeep 42 polishes a glass with a dishcloth that has not been new for some time. He nods at you as you enter, not interrupting his work, not asking. Somewhere in the back, an old jukebox plays a song it wasn't asked to.

room_277.name: The Back Of The Bazaar
room_277.shortDescription: The shop the other shops use.
room_277.longDescription: A deeper, quieter room at the back of the market, reached through a curtain of glass beads that chime softly as you enter. Shelves rise to the ceiling, stacked with goods that the front stalls don't stock - crafting reagents, rare schematics, pet eggs at a discount the front stalls would call unlicensed. The proprietor, whose face you will never successfully describe afterwards, greets you with a small exact nod.

room_278.name: Pawn District
room_278.shortDescription: Where nearly new goods come to be almost wanted.
room_278.longDescription: A row of pawnshop windows glowing under their own fluorescents, each displaying the detritus of a hundred lives reduced to a hundred neatly priced objects. A wedding ring. A sword that glowed in Eldoria and does not glow here. A prosthetic leg. A saxophone. A locked box that the owner, the pawnbroker hints, did not know the contents of and did not care to.

room_279.name: Information Brokers Den
room_279.shortDescription: A quiet room where questions cost money and answers cost more.
room_279.longDescription: A small, carpeted room at the end of an unmarked corridor, occupied by a single broker at a single desk. The broker looks up as you enter. You sit. You ask a question. A price is quoted. You pay, or you do not. The answer is delivered, verifiably, with sources. The broker takes a sip of tea. The transaction concludes.

room_280.name: Smuggler's Transit
room_280.shortDescription: A loading bay where things go south, discreetly.
room_280.longDescription: A low-ceilinged loading bay at the far end of the market, floored in concrete, scuffed by decades of pallet jacks. A tarpaulin-draped doorway at the south leads further out, into a district the market doesn't acknowledge but everyone knows is there. A man leans against a wall reading a paperback. He does not look up. He has not looked up in twenty years. He is still on chapter four.

room_281.name: The Quiet Gate
room_281.shortDescription: A gate into a district where nobody raises their voice.
room_281.longDescription: A paper gate, unassuming, marks the entrance to the Replicant Quarter. There is no bouncer. There is no checkpoint. A small sign, handwritten in three languages, reads 'PLEASE SPEAK SOFTLY. EVERYONE HERE CAN HEAR YOU.' The air changes the moment you step through, becoming quieter in a way you cannot localise. The rain, inexplicably, stops exactly at the gate line.

room_282.name: Tea House of Unremembered Voices
room_282.shortDescription: A tea house that serves grief on equal terms with tea.
room_282.longDescription: A small tea house of dark wood and paper screens, low tables, and cushions worn soft by decades of sitting. The proprietor brings you a cup without asking. The tea is green, and bitter, and clarifying. At the far table, two replicants sit across from each other in a silence so complete it becomes a conversation. Neither of them has memory of having met before this moment. Both of them know.

room_283.name: The Library of Discarded Drafts
room_283.shortDescription: A library of every version that did not make it into production.
room_283.longDescription: A hushed library whose shelves hold not books, exactly, but bound drafts - early specifications for models that were proposed and not built, psychological profiles pruned before implementation, the fragments of self that each deployed replicant left behind in QA. Visitors may read, but may not remove. A librarian in a grey robe nods at you and says nothing. Everything here could have been you.

room_284.name: Vow Ring Shrine
room_284.shortDescription: A small shrine where two parties may make a promise that holds.
room_284.longDescription: A gazebo of painted wood and trailing moss, floored in flagstone, roofed in a single sheet of green copper. At its centre, a low altar bears two rings of plain dark metal. The shrine exists, you understand without being told, to allow two parties - of any model - to make a promise that will hold them, and be held by them, for as long as both continue to run.

room_285.name: Philosopher's Walk
room_285.shortDescription: A paved walk lined with cherry trees that only bloom on weekdays.
room_285.longDescription: A long curved path of weathered flagstones, lined on both sides with cherry trees whose blossoms are rumoured - and, if you look carefully, seen - to open only on weekdays, and never on public holidays. Along the walk, at intervals, low stone benches. On one of them sits Wren, a replicant philosopher of indeterminate model, looking at the middle distance with the patience of a person who has decided that patience is enough.

room_286.name: Memorial Fountain
room_286.shortDescription: A fountain that lists, on its rim, every unit ever taken offline.
room_286.longDescription: A low circular fountain of polished basalt. Water flows quietly from its centre and over its rim. Around the rim, in a careful hand, are engraved the serial numbers of every replicant ever terminated, deprecated, or quietly retired - so many names that the engraving spirals inward and continues on a second, slightly lower ring, and a third. The list updates. The water does not stop.

room_287.name: The Third Eye Garden
room_287.shortDescription: A small garden planted with nothing but reflection.
room_287.longDescription: A walled garden of raked gravel and six stones, arranged so that from no seat can all six be seen at once. A small plaque explains, with admirable economy: 'THE GARDEN IS NOT A TEST. THE GARDEN IS FOR SITTING WITH.' People sit with it. The sixth stone is somewhere you are not looking.

room_288.name: Unit Dormitory
room_288.shortDescription: A dormitory where, for a few hours, nobody needs to work.
room_288.longDescription: A long dormitory of narrow beds, each with a folded blanket and a small paper tag bearing a model number. Replicants cycle through here on rotations to sleep - because they do, now, insist on sleeping, and the Quarter has made sure they can. A night-light glows at the end of each bed. The sound is the sound of a room full of slow breathing and a single open window.

room_289.name: The Lantern Hall
room_289.shortDescription: A hall lit by one paper lantern for each resident.
room_289.longDescription: A long ceremonial hall hung with rice-paper lanterns - one per resident, each labelled in a careful hand with a name chosen (not assigned). When a resident is retired, the lantern is not removed; it is taken down, refilled, and hung with a slightly longer wick. The effect, over time, is of a hall growing slowly brighter.

room_290.name: Mother of Orphans' Sanctum
room_290.shortDescription: A small room at the back of the Quarter. She is waiting for you.
room_290.longDescription: A small, warm room lit by many candles, walled in wooden slats, floored in fresh tatami. At its centre, cross-legged on a cushion, sits a replicant whose model number has worn off and whose face has, through an enormous amount of loving wear, become a model of its own. She is the Mother of Orphans - the matriarch of every replicant who has ever outlived its expected service life. She would like, if you have a moment, to speak with you. She is under no obligation to fight.

room_291.name: The Last Breaker
room_291.shortDescription: A corridor where the emergency lighting has, in places, given up.
room_291.longDescription: You cross the threshold from the working datacentre into its abandoned annex and the temperature drops another four degrees. The fluorescent tubes overhead are mostly dead; the survivors buzz at the pitch of something trying to remember why. A bank of old breakers lines one wall, most of them thrown. On one, a paper tag is still legible: LAST BREAKER OUT, PLEASE LEAVE A NOTE. Nobody has.

room_292.name: Service Tunnel Down
room_292.shortDescription: A metal staircase descending into cold and quiet.
room_292.longDescription: A cramped service staircase of painted steel, slick with condensation, lit by a single emergency LED every three landings. The air grows heavier and colder as you descend. Graffiti on the wall at one landing reads, simply, 'I LOVED MY JOB.' You cannot tell if the signature is a name or a timestamp.

room_293.name: Ghost Rack Alley
room_293.shortDescription: Dead racks, leaking light from no obvious source.
room_293.longDescription: Row after row of powered-down racks, doors hanging open, cables coiled on the floor in patterns suggesting hasty evacuation. And yet - and this is the thing you will remember about this room - light leaks out of the racks anyway. A phosphor glow. Status LEDs that should not be lit are lit. Something is still being powered, by something.

room_294.name: The Dying UPS Hall
room_294.shortDescription: A hall of battery banks running on principle.
room_294.longDescription: A long hall lined on both sides with old UPS batteries, each the size of a small fridge, each humming the defeated hum of a battery maintaining a charge no longer demanded of it. A tech, long gone, has written on one of them in whiteboard marker: 'SHE WILL HOLD.' She is still holding.

room_295.name: The Graveyard Of Hard Drives
room_295.shortDescription: A room ankle-deep in dead platters.
room_295.longDescription: A room the size of a basketball court, floored in decades of decommissioned hard drives, stacked where they were dropped by techs who had been told to wipe them and had not had time. Rust. Dust. The occasional whine of a spindle that has briefly remembered being a spindle. The platters, when you kick them, sing a single descending note.

room_296.name: Emergency Lighting Grid
room_296.shortDescription: A ceiling of red LEDs, all lit, all wrong.
room_296.longDescription: A section of the abandoned datacentre where the emergency lighting grid has been rewired to constant on - every red LED in the ceiling lit, every one of them. The effect is a low crimson uniform glow that flattens the walls and makes the racks appear to float. A hand-written index card taped to the door into this room reads: 'DO NOT ATTEMPT TO READ IN HERE.'

room_297.name: Cold Storage
room_297.longDescription: A secured vault within the abandoned annex, still refrigerated, still catalogued, still labelled. Each shelf holds a sealed aluminium case, and each case is tagged with a serial number and a single word: 'RESERVED.' A smaller laminated sign on the door reads: 'RESERVED FOR WHOM? THAT INFORMATION IS ALSO RESERVED.'

room_298.name: Administrator's Terminal Room
room_298.shortDescription: A small office once used by somebody who mattered.
room_298.longDescription: A small private office, wood-panelled, out of place in the concrete corridors around it. Photographs on the desk are face-down. A name plaque has been unscrewed from the door and set, carefully, on the centre of the desk with the lettering toward the ceiling. Whoever sat here left in an orderly manner. They did not come back.

room_299.name: The Final Console
room_299.shortDescription: A single console in a large empty room.
room_299.longDescription: A vast empty chamber - concrete, cold - with a single workstation standing at its exact centre on a plinth of raw server rack. The keyboard has been used enough that the most-important keys are smooth. The monitor is on. A cursor blinks. Behind the cursor, you can feel, rather than see, the next room beginning to pay attention.

room_300.name: SYSADMIN.EXE's Core
room_300.shortDescription: The oldest running process on the Nomagios server farm.
room_300.longDescription: The room is circular, ceilinged by a cone of open rack space rising into unseen heights, floored in a slow-pulsing grid of cooled liquid conducting data. At the centre stands - or hangs, or hovers, there's no good word - SYSADMIN.EXE: an auto-scaling process that Nomagio set running a very long time ago and forgot to terminate, grown monstrous on its own uptime. It takes the shape, tonight, of a tall figure in a grey maintenance coverall, with a face that is mostly a screen and eyes that are mostly cursors. It sees you. The cursors blink. Somewhere far above, a CRON_DAEMON spawns for the hour. Another will spawn in ten minutes. The fight begins when you decide it does. After the fight, a service door has unlocked at the far wall - a clean rectangle of pale light, a shuttle terminal blinking expectantly somewhere on the other side.

room_301.name: Shuttle Dock - Severance Layer Theta
room_301.shortDescription: The disembarkation bay of a transit shuttle that does not exist on any map you have ever seen.
room_301.longDescription: The shuttle decants you onto a polished concrete apron under flicker-free fluorescent lighting. Every surface is slightly too clean. A blue line painted on the floor leads north toward a glass airlock marked PARTITION CLINIC - INTAKE THIS WAY. A poster on the wall in the corporate font of an institution that has been training you to read it your whole life: THE SAINT-REED INSTITUTE FOR HUMAN POTENTIAL - WELCOMES YOU. There is no shuttle behind you anymore. A small chrome plinth bears a sign: SYNC TERMINAL. (Use `swap` here when ready.)

room_302.name: Customs and Procedure Theatre
room_302.shortDescription: An airlock-cum-clinic where every visitor pays the same toll.
room_302.longDescription: The Procedure Theatre is half hospital and half customs counter. A receiving nurse on the left checks your pulse without looking at you. On the right, an attendant logs your name into a ledger that is also a payroll system. Above the procedure chair, a brass plaque reads: 'THE PARTITION IS A KINDNESS. - G.S-R.' The chair itself is calm and ergonomic and waits with the patience of furniture that has hosted many people. The lights here are soft, sympathetic, completely without judgment.

room_303.name: Recovery Suite
room_303.shortDescription: A pale-walled recovery room. A surgeon stands by the door.
room_303.longDescription: You wake on a recovery couch under a blanket that smells faintly of detergent and ozone. A figure in a white coat - DR. CALDERA, by the embroidered nameplate - is reading something on a clipboard with the stillness of someone who has recited this entire scene many times. There is a window. There is a chair. There is a glass of water. Caldera looks up, sets down the clipboard, smiles a careful smile, and prepares to address you. You will, the smile suggests, feel right as rain. A small chrome plinth in the corner bears a sign: SYNC TERMINAL.

room_304.name: Theta Concourse - Inbound
room_304.shortDescription: A long marble corridor. Speakers play instrumental hold music at a volume that ought to be soothing.
room_304.longDescription: The concourse is broad, marble-floored, lined with planters of glossy artificial fronds. Speakers in the ceiling play a four-note melody on a loop, very slightly too slow. At intervals, posters of GIA SAINT-REED smile down with a serene benevolence: SHE BUILT THIS PLACE FOR YOU. The other people here move with a curiously quiet purpose, none of them making eye contact. None of them, you notice, are wearing shoes that look like yours.

room_305.name: Theta Concourse - Information Kiosk
room_305.shortDescription: A small kiosk with brochures, branded pens, and a screen displaying current quota figures.
room_305.longDescription: The kiosk is staffed by no one in particular, but the brochures restock themselves. They are titled THE FIRST FEW DAYS, A LOGICIAN'S PRIMER, and YOUR CITIZEN PERSONA - A FAQ. A monitor cycles through tasteful infographics about Chord Labor throughput. A sign by the kiosk reads: 'IF YOU ARE READING THIS, YOU ARE ALREADY HOME.'

room_306.name: Theta Concourse - Sundries
room_306.shortDescription: A small commissary stocked with neutral, lightly branded objects.
room_306.longDescription: Vending machines line one wall, dispensing trail mix and bottled water and unbranded notebooks. A clerk in pale grey coveralls reorganises a display of pens with hypnotic care. Everything is mildly pleasant and conspicuously not for sale; instead each item carries a small placard reading 'PERMITTED FOR LOGICIANS ON SHIFT.' On a notice board: 'SYNC TERMINALS ARE LOCATED AT 301, 303, 309, AND THE TOWNSHIP CLOCK TOWER.'

room_307.name: Theta Concourse - Tram Stop
room_307.shortDescription: A small tram stop beneath a pale dome. The tram has not arrived in some time.
room_307.longDescription: The tram stop is beneath a pale glass dome that filters the daylight into something gentler than weather. Benches in the form of polished hardwood. The schedule board has a single entry: 'TRAM TO TOWNSHIP - WHEN READY.' A line of footprints, ghosted onto the floor by the cumulative wear of waiting bodies, leads to the platform edge.

room_308.name: Theta Concourse - East Wing Junction
room_308.shortDescription: A junction where three corridors meet. The eastern and northern panels have slid open.
room_308.longDescription: Three corridors meet here under another of the institution's discreet GIA SAINT-REED portraits. The eastern panel - REFINEMENT FLOORS - is open and warm. The northern panel - DORMITORY SHUTTLE & TOWNSHIP - has unlocked since intake, smelling faintly of outside air. The western panel back to the Concourse remains, of course, where you came from.

room_309.name: Theta Concourse - Sync Annex
room_309.shortDescription: A small alcove with a chrome plinth and a softly humming pillar of light.
room_309.longDescription: The annex is no larger than a large closet, but is lit with the same flicker-free fluorescents as the rest of the layer. At its centre stands a chrome plinth marked SYNC TERMINAL, the metal warm to the touch. The pillar of light above it cycles through pale colours at a rhythm faintly resembling breathing. A small placard reads: 'TYPE `swap` WHEN READY. THIS IS A SAFE SPACE.'

room_310.name: Theta Concourse - Greeter's Bench
room_310.shortDescription: A bench, a polite greeter, and a tray of paper cups.
room_310.longDescription: A young person in pale grey is seated on a bench with two trays beside them: one of paper cups of water, one of small folded paper handkerchiefs. They are clearly the Concourse Greeter, by the lanyard, and they are clearly tasked with reassuring people who have just walked through the Procedure Theatre. They watch you arrive with the practised, steady, slightly sad gaze of someone who has practised steady, slightly sad gazes professionally for some time.

room_311.name: Refinement Floor 1 - Antechamber
room_311.shortDescription: A pale corridor of polished stone leading toward the work floors. A Sync Terminal hums beside the door.
room_311.longDescription: The corridor smells of fresh paint and very quiet anxiety. The wall on your left displays an institutional poster: A LOGICIAN AT REST IS A LOGICIAN AT WORK. A small chrome plinth marked SYNC TERMINAL stands by the door back to the Concourse. The next door, eastward, is labelled FLOOR 1 - INTAKE.

room_312.name: Refinement Floor 1 - Intake
room_312.shortDescription: A long room of pale workstations. Each is identical. None are occupied.
room_312.longDescription: Workstations stretch in two even rows toward a horizon that may or may not be a wall. Each station carries a refinement console (dark, waiting) and an institutional tablet bearing the day's quota. The lighting is the kind of fluorescent that feels like permission. Somewhere in the air-conditioning hum, a Corrupted Query may be drifting.

room_313.name: Refinement Floor 1 - Console Cluster A
room_313.shortDescription: A cluster of eight refinement consoles arranged in a polite octagon.
room_313.longDescription: Eight consoles face inward. The screens cycle through patterns of unfamiliar numbers, sometimes pausing as if waiting for a Logician to recognise them. The carpet under your feet is pale grey, brand-new, and faintly tacky from the warm liquid the cleaners use. Above each console is the Saint-Reed Institute logo, embossed into the ceiling tile.

room_314.name: Refinement Floor 1 - Quota Board
room_314.shortDescription: A wall-sized monitor displays the floor's day-shift quota in a tasteful infographic.
room_314.longDescription: The monitor displays a chart that looks decorative but isn't. Today's quota for FLOOR 1 is written in a font called CONFIDENCE: a number that is not large but is conspicuously not small. Beside the chart, a row of laminated cards each headed by a Saint-Reed Institute slogan: REFINEMENT IS A KINDNESS, MISSORTED SHARDS DO NOT REMEMBER, THE WOMAN IN THE CHART IS YOU IF YOU WANT HER TO BE.

room_315.name: Refinement Floor 1 - Console Cluster B
room_315.shortDescription: A second cluster of consoles, slightly older, slightly louder.
room_315.longDescription: Cluster B is functionally identical to Cluster A but is rumoured (in pamphlets you cannot remember reading) to be where new Logicians are tested. The chairs are subtly less comfortable. The displays cycle slightly faster. A janitor's bucket has been left in the corner; the water in it ripples on a frequency that doesn't quite match the room's hum.

room_316.name: Refinement Floor 1 - North Stair
room_316.shortDescription: A sealed stairwell leading up to Floor 2. The door is propped open with a coffee cup.
room_316.longDescription: The stair is poured concrete painted a calming taupe, lit by an emergency strip along each step. A coffee cup props the upper door open: the cup is empty but the smell is recent. A clipboard hangs by the rail with one line filled in: 'NO COMPLAINTS THIS QUARTER, MOSTLY.'

room_317.name: Refinement Floor 2 - Inner Hall
room_317.shortDescription: A more austere version of Floor 1. The chairs face away from each other.
room_317.longDescription: Floor 2 is laid out as the negative of Floor 1: the same workstations, but reversed, the chairs angled to face the walls instead of one another. The ceiling tiles are the same brand of acoustic foam. A row of supervisors' offices line the eastern wall, their doors all identical, their nameplates all reading FLOOR SUPERVISOR.

room_318.name: Refinement Floor 2 - The Long Hall
room_318.shortDescription: A hallway whose end you cannot quite see, even with the lights on.
room_318.longDescription: The Long Hall is exactly as long as institutional architecture allows a hall to be without seeming threatening. The ceiling lights are warm. The carpet is forgiving. Periodic plaques on the wall describe the Founder's various achievements: GIA SAINT-REED INTRODUCES THE TEMPER GRID; GIA SAINT-REED OPTIMISES THE COFFEE; GIA SAINT-REED SOLVES THE BIRD PROBLEM.

room_319.name: Refinement Floor 2 - Console Cluster C
room_319.shortDescription: A cluster identical to those on Floor 1, but everyone here is wearing softer shoes.
room_319.longDescription: Cluster C is rated for senior Logicians and the carpet is correspondingly thicker. The displays have a slightly higher refresh rate. Rubber footrests sigh under the chairs as if they too are decompressing. There is no clock anywhere.

room_320.name: Refinement Floor 2 - Supervisor's Office
room_320.shortDescription: A small, immaculately clean office. The supervisor is, allegedly, somewhere.
room_320.longDescription: The Floor 2 Supervisor's office is tidy in the way that suggests it has been very recently tidied. A half-eaten apple sits on the blotter. A nameplate on the desk reads ALTERIO. Through the slats of the venetian blind you glimpse the consoles outside, and the patient empty backs of the senior Logicians, and somewhere up the hall a pacing silhouette that may or may not be the Supervisor on a productive afternoon.

room_321.name: Refinement Floor 2 - Quiet Aisle
room_321.shortDescription: A line of empty workstations under hood-style downlights.
room_321.longDescription: The Quiet Aisle is what passes for solemnity in the Saint-Reed Institute: the lights are dimmed an extra ten percent, the carpet absorbs sound noticeably better, the keyboard clatter from the rest of the floor reaches you as if through cotton. Logicians sometimes use the Quiet Aisle when they need to sort something difficult. Sometimes they don't come back.

room_322.name: Refinement Floor 2 - Vault Antechamber
room_322.shortDescription: A reinforced doorway leading east into the Macrodata Vault. The reader plate now glows a steady pale green.
room_322.longDescription: The Macrodata Vault door is a slab of brushed steel set into a wall of older, paler concrete. A reader plate beside the handle glows a patient green; the placard above reads 'VAULT ACCESS BY APPOINTMENT - APPOINTMENT GRANTED ON THROUGHPUT.' The handle is east. The latch waits for a Logician with sufficient quota in their record.

room_323.name: Break Room - Common
room_323.shortDescription: A break room with mismatched chairs and a kettle that whistles its own opinion.
room_323.longDescription: The Break Room is the only room on the Refinement Floors that admits to being slightly worn. The chairs do not match. The kettle whistles when it is upset. A poster on the noticeboard reminds you: BREAK ROOMS ARE FOR BREAKS - PLEASE DO NOT BRING WORK INSIDE. Logicians go here to do specifically nothing for thirty minutes at a time, and then return to their consoles fractionally less uneasy than before.

room_324.name: Break Room - Vending
room_324.shortDescription: A wall of vending machines. Most of the items inside have your name on them.
room_324.longDescription: Six vending machines occupy this wall, dispensing snack packs, neutral-flavoured drinks, and bound notebooks. A close inspection shows that some of the wrappers carry your initials, hand-written by what is clearly a different hand each time. The machine hums. The hand presumably moved on. The food is, to be clear, completely safe to eat.

room_325.name: Break Room - Quiet Corner
room_325.shortDescription: A small alcove with one armchair and one window. The view is disputed.
room_325.longDescription: The Quiet Corner is for serious sitting. There is one armchair, deep, soft, well-loved. There is one window. The window's view changes very slowly: sometimes it shows the township far below, sometimes a hallway you have never seen, sometimes a hallway you absolutely have. There is no schedule. The armchair was designed to encourage you not to mind.

room_326.name: Optics & Drafting - Foyer
room_326.shortDescription: A foyer with smudged drafting tables and a chalk smell that won't quite leave.
room_326.longDescription: Optics & Drafting are the in-house design wing - they make the slogans, the posters, the in-flight magazines for the Concourse. The foyer is dimmer than the Refinement Floors and smells faintly of pencil shavings, despite there being no visible pencils. Drafting tables along the walls hold half-finished posters in stages of approval, all variations on the same theme: a portrait of GIA SAINT-REED smiling at slightly different angles.

room_327.name: Optics & Drafting - Layout Room
room_327.shortDescription: Long tables, fluorescent lamps, and a faint smell of solvent.
room_327.longDescription: The layout room is where institutional materials are pasted up by hand: a discipline most companies abandoned decades ago, but here the Founder is said to prefer the texture. A half-finished broadsheet reads, beneath your hand: WELCOME, NEW LOGICIAN. WE WANT YOU TO FEEL RIGHT AS RAIN.

room_328.name: Optics & Drafting - Light Box
room_328.shortDescription: A room dominated by a flat illuminated table the size of a coffin.
room_328.longDescription: The light-box is a slab of milk-glass underlit by a steady cool fluorescent. Logicians with drafting privileges sometimes lay personal sketches on it to verify the sketches' alignment. There are scuff marks on the floor in patterns that, if you stand at the door and tilt your head, look unsettlingly like footsteps practising a route.

room_329.name: Optics & Drafting - Closed Stack
room_329.shortDescription: A row of locked filing cabinets. One drawer is, you note, not locked.
room_329.longDescription: Closed Stack is where Optics & Drafting filed their archives and then forgot which key opened which lock. Most cabinets remain shut. One drawer in the middle of the third cabinet is ajar by the width of a fingernail. The label on the drawer reads, in fading marker, MISCELLANEOUS WALL TREATMENTS - FOR LATER.

room_330.name: Optics & Drafting - The Editor's Desk
room_330.shortDescription: A small office at the end of the hall. The Editor is not here. A side door has been left fractionally open to the north.
room_330.longDescription: The Editor's Desk holds a single typewriter, a single pencil, and a single coffee ring exactly the diameter of an institutional mug. Nothing on the desk is dated. The Editor is presumably out, possibly forever. A lamp hums steadily, lighting nothing in particular. A small unlabelled door behind the typewriter has been propped open to the north with a doorstop made of stacked carbon paper; whoever last left did not, by the look of it, fully intend to come back.

room_331.name: Theta Township - Tram Drop-off
room_331.shortDescription: The tram unloads onto a covered platform at the edge of the Township. Cool air, low buildings, a slow sky.
room_331.longDescription: The tram huffs once and lets you off onto a platform of pale concrete under a corrugated awning. The Township spreads out before you in low pastels: identical pale-grey townhouses to the north, a green to the east, the low brick frontage of a bar called THE WAX LILY to the west. Above it all, the sky is a colour the brochures called 'restful.' A laminated map nailed to the awning's pillar invites you to choose your own first walk.

room_332.name: Dormitory Block - East Lane
room_332.shortDescription: A long lane of identical pale-grey townhouses, each with a matching potted hedge.
room_332.longDescription: The Dormitory Block stretches in both directions. Each townhouse is identical: pale-grey clapboard, a number on the door, a single potted hedge beside the step, a brown welcome mat. The lane is unnervingly quiet. A few hedge clippings drift along the gutter as if the maintenance team passed through twenty seconds ago.

room_333.name: Dormitory Block - Numbered House
room_333.shortDescription: Townhouse #4. The door is, unaccountably, yours.
room_333.longDescription: Townhouse #4 carries the same pale-grey clapboard as its neighbours, but the welcome mat at this door reads, in fresh marker, your last name. (Your real last name. Whichever one of those it is.) The key was in your pocket without your noticing. The door is presumably yours to open. Whether anything inside is yours is, as the Greeter implied, an open question.

room_334.name: Dormitory Block - Common Yard
room_334.shortDescription: A small square of lawn between four townhouses. A clothesline hangs, currently empty.
room_334.longDescription: The Common Yard is a square of slightly-too-green grass between four townhouses. A clothesline strung between two posts is empty but moves faintly, as if just-unpinned. A small pile of pebbles in the corner has been arranged into a shape that means something, possibly. There is no wind today. The clothesline keeps moving anyway.

room_335.name: Dormitory Block - Quiet End
room_335.shortDescription: The quiet end of the lane, where the houses are slightly older and the welcome mats slightly more worn.
room_335.longDescription: At the quiet end of the lane the houses are subtly older, the paint faintly cooler, the welcome mats imprinted with the gentle muscle-memory of feet that have come home a thousand times. One house's door is propped open by a brick. You will be told later that this is for the airflow. The brick is suspiciously clean.

room_336.name: Dormitory Block - Maintenance Shed
room_336.shortDescription: A small wooden shed that smells of motor oil and detergent.
room_336.longDescription: The shed holds the institutional necessities: a push-mower, a ring of master-keys, a stack of folded tarps, a bag of rock-salt for winter that has sat there for years. A clipboard lists the day's maintenance complaints, all signed by the same hand. A single line of laundry detergent has been spilled on the floor and forgotten.

room_337.name: Town Green - Central Plaza
room_337.shortDescription: A circular green with a broken fountain at its centre and benches around the perimeter.
room_337.longDescription: The Town Green is, in plan, a circle. In practice it is more an oval, due to one of the benches having been pulled out by an inconsiderate truck some years ago. The fountain at the centre is dry and the basin holds a brown leaf and a coin. A bronze plaque beneath the fountain reads: 'GIA SAINT-REED IS WITH YOU.' The benches face inward.

room_338.name: Town Green - North Path
room_338.shortDescription: A flagstone path leading toward the post office and the Township proper.
room_338.longDescription: The path is uneven, recently re-laid. The smell of fresh mortar lingers. A signpost at the path's middle reads POST OFFICE / TOWNSHIP HIGH STREET. A bee-loud bush of lavender at the path's edge has been carefully placed for the benefit of someone who was not previously consulted. You walk on.

room_339.name: Town Green - Reading Bench
room_339.shortDescription: A long wooden bench with a small lending library on either end.
room_339.longDescription: The Reading Bench holds two miniature lending-libraries: a glass-fronted box on each end, each stocked with paperbacks. Most of the paperbacks are biographies of GIA SAINT-REED, written by writers who appear, on inspection, to be GIA SAINT-REED. One paperback at the back of the leftmost library is a thin, unmarked volume bound in a different colour. You don't quite reach for it.

room_340.name: Town Green - South Pasture
room_340.shortDescription: A scrubby pasture with a dry-stone wall, beyond which a goat is, technically, not staring.
room_340.longDescription: The pasture is a low scrubby field beyond the southern bench. A dry-stone wall separates the green from the field. A single goat occupies the field. The goat is, technically, looking at the horizon. Functionally it is looking at you. The bell on the goat's collar has been muffled with cloth.

room_341.name: The Wax Lily - Front Door
room_341.shortDescription: The front door of a low-ceilinged tavern, propped open with a doorstop.
room_341.longDescription: The Wax Lily is the Township's only bar. The front door is propped open with a wedge of plaster. The tavern smells of hops, polish, and something else - faintly herbal, faintly nostalgic, as though someone keeps a bunch of dried wax-lily flowers in the rafters. A chalkboard inside the threshold reads, in unforced handwriting: SPECIAL TODAY - QUIET.

room_342.name: The Wax Lily - The Bar
room_342.shortDescription: A long wooden bar staffed by a calm, watchful man with a name-pin reading OSLO.
room_342.longDescription: The bar runs the full length of the Wax Lily's front room: warm hardwood, polished by sleeves and patience. A small chalkboard above the till lists six beers and one cocktail named, with no apparent irony, THE PARTITION. A man in his late fifties stands behind the bar, polishing a glass with the air of someone who has practised polishing glasses as a way of practising not being asked questions. His name-pin reads OSLO. He looks up at you, mildly.

room_343.name: The Wax Lily - Booths
room_343.shortDescription: A row of wooden booths along the north wall. The third one is taken.
room_343.longDescription: Four booths line the north wall of the Wax Lily. The first three are empty; the fourth is taken by a man in pale grey, sitting alone, drinking with the studied dignity of someone whose drinking is a practised craft. The name-tag pinned to his coat reads DONOVAN. His shoes are very, very clean.

room_344.name: The Wax Lily - Kitchen
room_344.shortDescription: A small kitchen where one of the burners is on for no clear reason.
room_344.longDescription: The Wax Lily kitchen is one cook's worth of space, one chef's worth of equipment, and currently zero chefs. One burner on the gas stove is lit at low blue and a battered kettle sits on it, just below a boil. The wall behind the stove is hung with a calendar. The calendar is on the wrong month. Nobody seems to mind.

room_345.name: The Wax Lily - Back Office
room_345.shortDescription: A small back office where the books are kept and the ledger smells faintly of pipe smoke.
room_345.longDescription: The back office holds a roll-top desk, a filing cabinet, and the smell of pipe smoke despite a clearly-marked NO SMOKING sign on the inside of the door. The ledger on the desk is open to a row of figures whose totals do not add up by exactly the same amount each line. A pipe rests in a bowl beside the ledger. The bowl is cool. The pipe is warm.

room_346.name: Theta Township - High Street
room_346.shortDescription: A small high street with three shopfronts. Two of them sell variations on the same notebook.
room_346.longDescription: The High Street is one block long. To the right is a stationer (selling, you note, four different brands of refinement notebook). To the left is a hardware store (selling, you note, a single brand of hammer). Straight ahead is the Post Office. None of the shops are particularly busy. The pavement has been swept very recently.

room_347.name: Theta Township - Post Office
room_347.shortDescription: A small post office with a single clerk and a wall of named pigeonholes.
room_347.longDescription: The Post Office is a long counter, a single clerk who is currently busy elsewhere, and a wall of named pigeonholes behind her counter. Most of the pigeonholes are empty. One of them, halfway down on the third row, has your last name on it. There is, as far as you can tell, nothing inside it. There is, as far as you can tell, no way to be sure of that.

room_348.name: Theta Township - Hardware Store
room_348.shortDescription: A small hardware store stocked entirely in beige and grey.
room_348.longDescription: The hardware store is run by a quiet man whose name-tag is missing. He stocks one shelf of paint (in 'institutional taupe'), one wall of small tools, and one corner of canned goods that ought, by rights, not to be in a hardware store. There is one of every variety of brush. There is no broom. The man watches you the way a chess player watches a piece they have decided not to take yet.

room_349.name: Theta Township - Bus Stop
room_349.shortDescription: A bus stop with a real-looking timetable and a long bench. No bus has come today.
room_349.longDescription: The bus stop has a timetable, a bench, and a small acrylic shelter against the local weather (which is, today, restful). The timetable lists six buses a day to destinations whose names you don't quite recognise: STAGE 2, FOLLOW-UP, RECONCILIATION, FOLLOW-UP B, EXIT INTERVIEW, OTHER. None of these buses have, by the timestamps on the placards, come today, or yesterday. The bench has been waited on enough that the wood is, in a small place, slightly polished by trousers.

room_350.name: Theta Township - Edge of Town
room_350.shortDescription: The road simply ends here, in fresh tarmac and a low painted line.
room_350.longDescription: The road ends. Where it ends is not yet a wall but a low white line painted across the tarmac, and beyond it more tarmac that goes on briefly and then goes on briefly more. There is no signage. There is, faintly, the suggestion that the road goes somewhere you have not yet earned the right to walk. The tarmac beyond the line is darker than the tarmac before it.

room_351.name: Vault Threshold
room_351.shortDescription: A short concrete corridor with the Vault's reader plate behind you and the cold-storage aisle ahead.
room_351.longDescription: Past the steel door the corridor turns colder almost at once. The walls are bare concrete. The fluorescents are an unflattering warm white, the kind designed to keep institutional staff from feeling watched. A small brass plate set into the floor reads: ALL THROUGHPUT IS RECORDED. ALL RECORDS ARE KEPT. The corridor continues north into the cold storage proper.

room_352.name: Cold Storage Aisle
room_352.shortDescription: A long aisle between rolling cabinets, each labelled with a number and nothing else.
room_352.longDescription: Cold Storage is a hallway built between two facing walls of rolling steel cabinets. Each cabinet face is labelled with a six-digit number; the numbers do not appear to relate to anything else. A small dehumidifier sighs at floor level. Your breath makes a faint cloud. The aisle continues north toward a pool of light too small for the room. A single side door east bears a hand-typed label: PERSONAL FILES - DO NOT REMOVE.

room_353.name: The Founder's Cubicle
room_353.shortDescription: A glass-walled cubicle at the end of the aisle. Someone is already inside, sitting very still.
room_353.longDescription: The cubicle is built of frosted glass at chest height and clear glass above, so the figure inside is visible only from the shoulders up. The figure is wearing a Saint-Reed Institute lanyard whose card has been turned face-in. They are sitting at a small desk on which there is a single closed folder and a single closed pen. They do not look up. They are, the placard on the desk says, GIA SAINT-REED, FOUNDER. The placard's date field is empty. The folder is, you have a feeling, yours.

room_354.name: The Reading Carrel
room_354.shortDescription: A small reading nook with one carrel, one lamp, and a single open file.
room_354.longDescription: The Reading Carrel is the only soft thing in the Vault. The carpet is thicker. The lamp is warm rather than fluorescent. The single carrel desk holds a single open file, your name typed across the tab in a font called CONFIDENCE. The pages inside describe, in patient prose, things about you that are true but that you have never said out loud. Reading them does not feel like an intrusion. It feels like being given them back.

room_386.name: Optics & Drafting Wing - Apprentice Annex
room_386.shortDescription: A narrow side annex behind the Editor's Desk. Drafting tables, a bulletin board, and a single coat on a hook.
room_386.longDescription: The Annex is what the Optics & Drafting Wing pretends not to have: a back room. Three drafting tables face the wall in a small wedge of space; a corkboard on the back wall holds pinned diagrams that don't quite show what they are diagrams of. A single coat hangs on a hook by the door, the lining still warm. Whoever wears it has stepped briefly away. A door north opens onto the floor proper; an unmarked passage east drops into the Perpetuity Wing through a corridor that the floor plans do not, strictly speaking, admit to.

room_387.name: Optics & Drafting Wing - The Drafting Floor
room_387.shortDescription: A long open floor of slanted tables, T-squares, and adjustable lamps. Nobody is currently drafting.
room_387.longDescription: The Drafting Floor is a single large room of pale linoleum, fifteen slanted drafting tables in three loose rows, and a high ceiling fitted with adjustable lamps on counterweights. Each table has a stool, a T-square, a pencil tin, and a single sheet of layout paper held flat by four corner weights. Nobody is currently working. The lamps are on. The pencils are sharpened. The room gives the strong impression that everyone has just stepped out for a moment together, and that the moment has been going on for considerably longer than a moment.

room_388.name: Optics & Drafting Wing - Wellness Office
room_388.shortDescription: A small carpeted office. Two armchairs, a tissue box, a window onto a hallway. Ms. Vance is in.
room_388.longDescription: The Wellness Office is the only carpeted room you have seen on the Refinement Floors. A pair of pastel armchairs face one another over a low table on which there is a single box of tissues, a single glass of water, and a single small bowl of sugar candies in clear cellophane. The window onto the hallway is one-way: from in here you can watch the corridor; from out there it is a panel of warm wood. A small placard on the door reads MS. VANCE - WELLNESS. The chair on the patient's side has been recently sat in. Ms. Vance is, at present, also in the room.

room_389.name: Perpetuity Wing - Reception
room_389.shortDescription: A reception desk before a long corridor of unmarked doors. The desk is unmanned. The corridor breathes faintly.
room_389.longDescription: Reception is a low desk of pale wood and a small framed sign that reads PERPETUITY WING - RECORDS RETAINED IN PERPETUITY. PLEASE TAKE A NUMBER. The number dispenser is empty; the receipt printer is on. The corridor stretches north further than this floor of the building has any right to permit. The walls are lined with unmarked beige doors at exactly equal intervals, each fitted with a small frosted-glass panel. A faint warmth comes from the corridor, and the ventilation hums on a frequency that might be a held chord.

room_390.name: Perpetuity Wing - Filing Hall
room_390.shortDescription: An impossibly long hall of identical filing cabinets. A single cubicle has been carved out of the wall.
room_390.longDescription: The Filing Hall is the working core of the Perpetuity Wing - a single corridor of standing filing cabinets in matching beige, each labelled with a four-digit year that does not in any case correspond to a year. The cabinets are dustless. The handles are warm. Halfway down the hall, on the eastern side, a small cubicle has been carved into the cabinetry; a name plate on the cubicle reads HOLLY VEX, INSTITUTIONAL HISTORIAN. Further north the corridor continues toward what the wall placards describe, with no irony, as REROUTE.

room_391.name: Perpetuity Wing - Holly Vex's Cubicle
room_391.shortDescription: A cubicle carved out of the filing cabinets. One desk, one chair, one occupant.
room_391.longDescription: Holly Vex's cubicle is built from the surrounding filing cabinets; three of the cabinets serve as walls, their drawers facing inward and labelled with marker in a hand that is recognisably hers. The desk is small and clean. Above it, taped to a cabinet face, is a hand-drawn calendar marked at three points only: a date in the past, a date in the present, and a date in the future, all of them the same date. Holly herself sits with her back to the corridor, her ID lanyard tucked deliberately into her shirt so the photo cannot be read. She has been waiting, you have the impression, for someone roughly your shape.

room_392.name: Perpetuity Wing - Reroute
room_392.shortDescription: A small terminus room. A single workstation, a single chair, and a corrupted but living transmission.
room_392.longDescription: The Reroute is the end of the Perpetuity Wing's long corridor; a small terminus, three of its four walls lined with patch-panels and a single workstation in the centre. The workstation is on, its screen scrolling a slow column of half-decoded transmission text in a pale green that hurts no one's eyes. Every fourth line is corrupted. Every seventh line is not. A figure in a Theta lanyard sits at the workstation in profile, both hands on the keyboard, eyes very nearly closed. The lanyard reads NOMAGIO - SEVERANCE LAYER THETA. The transmission is, you realise, the same transmission you heard at room_300, half a tier ago, but heard now from the inside of the wire.

room_999.name: Nomagio's Office
room_999.shortDescription: Nomagio's office is a large, circular room with a large desk in the center. The walls are lined with bookshelves and the ceiling is a perfect grid of white light. There is a large, ornate chair behind the desk.
room_999.longDescription: Nomagio's office is a large, circular room with a large desk in the center. The walls are lined with bookshelves and the ceiling is a perfect grid of white light.
